advantages: Split-prefixed metrics (train/, val/, test/) make overfitting visible when train curves improve while validation curves stall or worsen.
Scalar curves over epochs or steps give a time-based view of learning— faster than relying on a single end-of-run number.
Consistent naming across runs lets experiment trackers overlay and rank hyperparameter sweeps without manual relabeling.
Logging a designated primary validation metric (val/f1, val/rmse) focuses model selection on the task-relevant score.
Separating train, validation, and test metrics preserves the locked-test principle in log form— test/* appears at sign-off, not in every training step.
disadvantages: Too many logged scalars (dozens per step) inflate storage and slow dashboards without improving decisions.
Noisy validation curves from small val sets can mislead early stopping and checkpoint selection if read without smoothing or confidence checks.
Train metrics alone look optimistic— teams that rank by train/accuracy often pick overfit models unless validation is enforced.
Inconsistent naming between teammates or scripts breaks cross-run comparison until logs are normalized retroactively.
Logging test metrics every epoch creates social pressure to tune on test curves— undermining the purpose of a locked test set.
